abstract.para: systemd-coredump provides a centralized, automated framework for capturing and managing process crashes by intercepting kernel signals to store compressed memory images and metadata, which are then easily analyzed through the coredumpctl utility.

topic.para: systemd-coredump is enabled by default. The primary configuration file is located at /etc/systemd/coredump.conf.
The following parameters in [Coredump] define how dumps are handled:

listitem.para: Defines where to store dumps such as none, external, journal. The default is external.
Enables/disables compression for the stored core dump. By default, this is enabled.
The maximum size of a core dump to be processed. The default size is 2G
The maximum size for a dump stored on disk. The default size is 2G

The maximum size of core dumps that can be stored directly within the system journal (when Storage=journal is used). If a core dump exceeds this value, it will be ignored or handled according to other storage settings.

The maximum amount of disk space the coredump storage (typically /var/lib/systemd/coredump/) is allowed to occupy. Once this limit is reached, older core dumps are automatically deleted to make room for new ones.

The minimum amount of free disk space that must be maintained on the filesystem where core dumps are stored. systemd-coredump will stop saving new dumps if doing so would cause the available disk space to fall below this threshold.

topic.para: Size units are B, K, M, G, T, P, and E. The ExternalSizeMax also supports a value of infinity.

note.para: A new instance of systemd-coredump is invoked for every crash. Configuration changes are applied immediately to the next crash; you do not need to restart any services.

section.title: Managing Core Dump Storage
section.para: To minimize the storage of sensitive information, you can disable disk storage and log only to the journal by modifying /etc/systemd/coredump.conf. In systemd configuration, if a field is explicitly set to nothing or if the feature is disabled via none, the system defaults to external, logging only the basic event details to systemd-journald. You can control this behavior in /etc/systemd/coredump.conf with the Storage option:

listitem.para: Storage=none Dumps are logged in the journal but the memory image is not saved to disk.
Storage=journal Cores are stored directly within the systemd journal.

section.para: Depending on the storage configuration, core dumps may not be preserved after a system restart (for example, if stored in volatile journal memory or a temporary directory). To ensure a core dump is saved to a permanent file for later analysis:
For more information, see man systemd-coredump, man coredumpctl, man core, and man coredump.conf for complete command and option listing.
